one_line_takeaway: Non-security ML paper: defines and theoretically/empirically validates 'plasticity' (average input-output rate of change) of ViT submodules, showing that finetuning the least-smooth components (attention, then feedforward layers) consistently beats finetuning the smoothest ones (LayerNorms) across 11 benchmarks and ~1000 runs.
key_finding: Empirical plasticity ranking (MHA highest, then FC1, FC2, LN2, LN1 lowest) matches the theoretical ranking and holds across ViT-Base, ViT-Huge, DINOv3, and GPT2. In the finetuning experiments (Table 1), average top-1 accuracy across 11 benchmarks was: MHA 90.8%, FC1 90.7%, FC2 90.3%, LN2 89.9%, LN1 89.8% -- i.e., finetuning the highest-plasticity component (attention) statistically significantly outperformed finetuning the lowest-plasticity components (LayerNorms) per a Wilcoxon signed-rank test at 5% confidence (all comparisons except FC1 vs MHA were significant). High-plasticity components also showed steadier accuracy across learning rates/seeds and exhibited larger, more stable gradient norms and steeper loss descent during training (Fig. 5). As a side result, finetuning only the LayerNorms with Adam reached 89.4% accuracy on Cifar100 using 28K trainable parameters, versus a cited LoRA baseline (Bafghi et al. 2024, Table 1) of 88.1% with 400K parameters -- i.e., ~15x fewer trainable parameters while outperforming that external LoRA baseline on that one benchmark.
core_contribution: The paper defines 'plasticity' of a vision transformer component as the average rate of change of its output with respect to input perturbations (P(f) = E[||f(x)-f(y)||_F / ||x-y||_F]), and shows it is the inverse notion of smoothness (a component with P(f) > 1 amplifies input differences on average and is 'non-smooth'; P(f) < 1 contracts them and is 'smooth'). The authors (1) derive theoretical upper bounds on plasticity for LayerNorm, feedforward linear layers, and multi-head self-attention (Propositions 1-4), producing a theoretical ranking MHA -> FC1 ~= FC2 -> LN2 ~= LN1; (2) empirically validate this ranking on pretrained ViT-Base, ViT-Huge, DINOv3 (7B, self-supervised), and GPT2 (124M, decoder-only), refining it to MHA -> FC1 -> FC2 -> LN2 -> LN1; and (3) run ~1000 finetuning experiments (component-wise finetuning in isolation, SGD and Adam optimizers, 4 learning rates x 3 seeds) on ViT-Base/Large/Huge across 11 image classification benchmarks, showing that finetuning high-plasticity components (attention, feedforward) yields higher and more stable accuracy than finetuning low-plasticity components (LayerNorms).
threat_model: Not applicable. This is not a security paper: it makes no claims about adversaries, attacks, defenses, or threat models. The paper does reference prior literature on 'adversarial robustness' and Lipschitz-constant regularization as motivating background for why smoothness has been considered desirable (citing Miyato et al. 2018, Jia et al. 2024, Tsuzuku et al. 2018), but these are cited related-work motivations, not something the paper itself studies, attacks, or defends against. The paper's own subject is transfer-learning/finetuning dynamics of ViT components.
limitations: The paper's own scope restrictions (as stated in the text): (1) components are finetuned strictly in isolation to avoid confounders, so the study does not address combinations of jointly-finetuned components, which the authors describe as 'computationally prohibitive' to explore exhaustively. (2) The theoretical attention bound (Proposition 3) requires bounded token norms (a ball of radius r), justified empirically/by prior work rather than proven in general; the tighter bound (Proposition 4) further assumes a bounded 'total image energy,' a signal-processing style assumption. (3) The self-attention module is only locally, not globally, Lipschitz continuous, so the bounds are conditional on the bounded-input regime observed in practice. (4) The authors explicitly do not claim a linear relationship between plasticity and downstream accuracy. (5) LLM/GPT2 and DINOv3 results are presented as a plasticity-pattern extension, not accompanied by the same large-scale finetuning-benefit experiments run for ViTs.

datasets_used: Cifar10-C (Contrast, Gaussian Noise, Motion Blur, Snow, Speckle Noise variants)
quantitative_results: Table 1 average top-1 accuracy over 11 benchmarks by finetuned component: MHA 90.8%, FC1 90.7%, FC2 90.3%, LN2 89.9%, LN1 89.8%
~1000 total finetuning runs (5 components x 4 learning rates x 3 seeds, times model/optimizer variants)
LayerNorm-only finetuning with Adam on Cifar100: 89.4% accuracy with 28K trainable parameters
Model scales studied: ViT-Base (86M params), ViT-Large (307M params), ViT-Huge (632M params), DINOv3 (7B params, self-supervised), GPT2 (124M params)
ViT-Huge sequence length n=257 vs ViT-Base n=197 (used to explain larger attention plasticity at larger scale per Proposition 3)
Plasticity measurement used 12,800 pretraining images embedded per experiment
baselines_compared: LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) -- cited external result from Bafghi et al. 2024 Table 1: 88.1% accuracy on Cifar100 with 400K trainable parameters, compared against the paper's own LayerNorm-only+Adam result of 89.4% with 28K parameters
Full finetuning baseline referenced via Touvron et al. 2022 (cited related work, not the paper's own experiment) for context on attention-only tuning surpassing full finetuning on small datasets
Linear probing accuracy used as the paper's own internal baseline for computing 'relative gain' percentages shown in Fig. 2
